Short Name of Idea/Solution: AI Agent to support Transaction Approvers
Description: Campus users have a painpoint around managing approvals. They are in disparate systems, with different push notifications (type and frequency) and source systems do not always have good search features that the users are looking for. We have a pending ITS request to build an application type solution for this, but maybe AI could do it instead. Systems could be in Oracle (different modules with different assignee structure, Concur, Kuali, ServiceNow, Ecotime, UCPath and more. I envision something like an agent where users can go to TGPT and type "show what my pending approvals are" and get the results live from the source systems. There are APIs for most (all?) of the requested approvals. See more detailed requirements here: https://ucsdcollab.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/IPAAS/pages/2513732904/FIS+Universal+Approval+Dashboard

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Use AI to match payment remittance information to open Invoices
Description: The current open AR receivables invoice amount is over $400M.  At the same time, the amount of payments at UCSD that are not yet applied to an invoice is over $90M.  Unapplied payments are a problem for the campus because cash needs to be applied for proper financial management and reporting at a department and enterprise level. AI could read the data on the unidentified payments and suggest likely matches with the AR data. Currently, the AR staff regularly review this data and attached to research and apply payments, but AI could speed up this process drastically. One of the root causes for unidentified payments is that customers send in payments with missing identifying information such as invoice number, customer number. There are also cases where the payment arrives before the invoice is processed and that problem is outside the scope of this request.  AI could also learn patterns of customers that AR staff could then reach out to fix the root issue. Other patterns of behavior could help with campus education and training to solve the root cause.

The Alumni team currently uses a platform, Gravyty, that handles their mentor matching to students. This platform will no longer exist in June 2026, and a replacement tool will be needed. They would like AI to automate and intelligently execute the mentor-mentee pairing process, replacing the functionality of the current costly, proprietary platform. The core problem being addressed is the imminent loss of the automated mentor-mentee matching functionality due to the sunsetting of the Triton's Connect (Gravyty) platform. The program involves recruiting approximately 800 students (mentees) and identifying about 300 alumni volunteers (mentors), resulting in approximately 375 matched students annually. The current platform is set to off-board on June 30.

The AI solution would specifically solve the need for a smart, automated match component between profiles for student and alumni mentors. The existing Triton's Connect platform uses a proprietary algorithm for matching, and this AI solution could possibly leverage internal technology (such as Triton GPT or a Gemini Gem) to replicate or improve this algorithm, creating a smarter and more efficient way to make pairings.
Ideally, it would take data gathered from mentor and mentee profiles/forms and run a comparison (potentially using sentiment analysis or common theme identification across 10-15 different points of interest) to generate viable matches.

IPPS Logistics is implementing a new, automated conveyor belt that takes high quality pictures and uses OCR to read package labels and route packages for delivery. Unfortunately, the OCR technology embedded within the conveyor isn't living up to its promise and we're going back and forth with them about the contract to fix it. While we do that, we engaged an AWS sponsored lab at CalPoly SLO to develop an AI powered Proof-of-Concept and they were able to deliver a solution that works much better. We would love it if this solution could be supported by TritonGPT/ UCSD AI.   See: https://ucsdcollab.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/IPPS/pages/2752479260/Logistics+-+CalPoly+Dx+Hub+AWS+OCR+Improvements+for+Conveyor+Belt

Planning to bring up at the conversation that's scheduled with OSI/Shawn Munro on 9/24, so documenting the idea formally here first. :)

Short Name of Idea/Solution: AI chatbot for Nano3 facility website
Description: I hope to have an AI chatbot that can be embedded to my unit's website, nanofab.ucsd.edu, for users to interact with and answer technical questions related to Nano3. At Nano3, we have many shared tools and many documents related to those tools. It will be great that the AI chatbot can be trained with our documents and then be able to provide answers to questions related to the equipment capability, process detail or limits, etc. Currently, the TritonGPT has a chatbot. But it is more for stuff on the Blink pages. It cannot answer questions specifically related to Nano3 and our tools.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: TritonGPT assistant to find faculty with specific skills or interests
Description:
In partnership with Ben Smarr (Assoc. Professor in Bioengineering / HDSI)

PROBLEM: Industry partners, students, media agents, legislators, and many others frequently reach out to contacts at UCSD seeking faculty with specific expertise. Additionally, ORI and only offices across campus similarly seek to identify faculty with specific expertise for research growth and research development opportunities. UCSD has no scalable solution for this need. Currently it requires tapping into knowledge held by individuals or inefficient bespoke searches that can be time consuming and incomplete. We propose an LLM trained on faculty content to efficiently and scalably address such queries. This will reduce burden on current faculty and staff, while improving speed and success connecting interested parties with the right UCSD faculty. 

PROJECT PROPOSAL: Industry partners, students, media agents, legislators, and many others frequently reach out to contacts at UCSD seeking faculty with specific expertise. UCSD has a large and growing number of faculty, and most departments are not up to date on the expertise in other parts of campus; many points of contact we have spoken with are clear that they often feel out of date even within their own department. At the same time, managing these demands imposes a substantial time cost, and opportunity cost when the right connections are not made. 

We propose developing an AI tool, driven by TritonGPT, to alleviate these costs while improving outcomes for such requests. To achieve this goal, we would first create scrapers for all departments to generate updated lists of all current faculty. Based on these lists, we will create scrappers to identify all publications, performances, and news mentions attributable to UCSD faculty. Text content will be downloaded, as will faculty webpage content. As an automated process, this database of content can be updated frequently and cheaply. Linking ORCID IDs to our scrapings will increase confidence for scientific publications that attribution is correct in those cases where faculty have common names. Infrequent requests for confirmation from faculty by email will further refine results when ambiguities are expected. Finally, we will develop an LLM layer with TritonGPT to enable common language queries to this database through a webpage portal.

In this way, someone might ask "who is doing HealthAI research related to cardiac disease?" and get back a list of faculty who best match this description, along with links to related content from within the database. This workflow could be expanded to include contact with relevant staff as well - for example, Prof. Smarr serves as the Industry Liaison Board Chair for HDSI, but works closely with Erik Mjoen on HDSI Staff, who has a full time position supporting industry relations. Someone making the above example query might then receive "Prof. Albert Hsaio at HDSI works on AI models for cardiac health. If you have questions about HDSI partnerships, Erik Mjoen is the lead for Industry Liaison Partners."

This work will elevate investments made in TritonGPT, will increase engagement between UCSD and extramural partners, and reduce burden on faculty and staff. It can be developed by student employees with faculty oversight, making it impactful, broadly useful across campus, and highly likely to be a net positive investment.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Using Generative AI to Gain Efficiencies in Academic Review and Academic File Preparation
Description:
Academic review at UC San Diego is a complex, labor-intensive, and time-consuming process.  It is multi-layered, involving many steps at many levels, and within those levels, work by different people and groups.  A critical first step to exploring the use of AI to increase ease and efficiency in academic review would be to determine which tasks, at which steps and levels, performed by which people, would be best targeted for a pilot intervention/program.  Key informants and stakeholders should be consulted and provide input on this question.  
It should be noted that the high-stakes nature of academic review means that the use of AI in this process is not without risks and requires close attention issues of transparency, privacy, and integrity.  A critical asset at UC San Diego that could be leveraged to potentially mitigate some risks is TritonGPT, our in house, generative AI platform that is securely hosted at the San Diego Supercomputer Center.  Notably, the TritonGPT development team has already built several “assistants” designed for use by University staff to help with administrative tasks.  These tools could serve as models for developing a sort of “academic review assistant”.
If risks could be mitigated, the potential benefits could be significant and the value proposition is high.  Reducing the labor and time involved in academic review could offer cost savings for the university and its schools and departments, as well as decreases in faculty burden related to preparation of file materials.

This idea is for a future extension/iteration of the idea submitted in 0153 for the OFC Workflow Solution focused on Professional Buyer Review. I understand there's a push from leadership to develop solutions towards "touchless administration". While we've done a lot to automate what we can of transactions - one part of transactions that still tend not be to automated (either based on departmental preference or by policy requirements) are Fund Manager/Financial Unit Manager review. 

With integrations to FinAH and Fund Manager Coach Assistant, I wonder if TritonGPT could be developed to do a review of transactional documents on behalf of Fund Managers - ex: POs, Invoices, Expense Reports, role requests, stipends, timecards, etc. Transactions that are high risk (TBD by document type and/or funding type?, or when budgets get close to max) or don't meet historical patterns could be routed for approval to a Fund Manager. But I bet a model like this could significantly cut down on workload all across campus, one one of our key populations that tend to be the most burdened transitionally.

Implementation could follow a similar model to what's been piloted in IPPS' proof of concept with the AWS team, an API pull of documents, run through AI engine, with an API sending  back a flag/field update that can be used as a workflow routing determinant in workflow. Would depend on the capabilities of each system - I know Oracle and Concur could support this, but can't speak to other systems.

Already discussed with OSI/ADIS/TritonGPT teams: 

IPPS has partnered with the the Digital Transformation Hub (DxHub), an AWS sponsored lab at CalPoly San Luis Obispo that helps public-sector agencies solve business problems using technology through lean prototyping projects their students take the lead in, to see if we can craft a smarter alternative for PO workflow than Oracle's limited If-Then rule engine using AI.

The DxHub has created a proof of concept for the AI evaluation of a PO and would like to share it with our local UCSD IT and discuss what consumption of the AI/implementation with Oracle via REST API could look like. 

Background documents: 
Original project proposal: see attached
Proposed Oracle Workflow and API design/interaction: https://ucsdcollab.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/IPPS/pages/2745892880/Proposed+Oracle+API+Workflow+Design
Attachments (optional): Cal Poly DxHub Challenge Submission Form_UCSDProfBuyerReview.docx

Description: Logistics has several processes currently on their Logistics.ucsd.edu portal, or Services and Support, where campus users contact them to initiate a manual process on the Logistics side - ex. Request to pickup a pickup a package instead of having it delivered, set up a new storage cage/locker, create/edit a water contract, etc). A good amount of theses processes could be automated with AI Agents that could review the request and do things like 1) review the open/available storage lockers and reserve the one that best meets the clients needs and draft a contract for the client, 2) set up an alert in our package tracking system to divert the package when it arrives for pickup instead of the standard delivery processing, 3) looking up the customers water account, and making the requisite edit. Complex orders could still be sent to to Logistic representative, but most of the requests could become more self-service giving customers faster responses, rather than waiting for the Logistics/warehouse staff to check in on these items once their back at their desks at the end of the day.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: AI Powered Request Form with AI Agents Complete Simple Tasks
Description: Residential, Retail, and Supply Chain Services/HDH has several service areas that have form based request processes - Conference Services, Guest Suites, Catering - that all have request forms online that then route to the teams to manually review and begin their manual workflow processes.  Instead, if workflow (AI supported or not) could help route the request based on complexity, simple requests could likely be handled by an AI Agent that could 1) check calendars, 2) create reservations, 3) create work orders in relevant systems (for food, AV, staff, etc), 4) send emails back to clients. Complex request that require back and forth with a client could still be routed to a human, but maybe event part of their process could be supported by AI Agents once they've worked out the necessary details with clients.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Use API with TritonGPT to Add Descriptions to Surplus Sales Images of Items for Sales
Description: Surplus Sales is the official campus reseller of university equipment. They list the items they sell on their own website (surplus.ucsd.edu) and also government auction site with images and product descriptions. These images and product descriptions are manually created/ entered, which can be time consuming. We've tried uploading images into TritonGPT and asking for it to generate product descriptions and it did reasonably well, but manually uploading the images and copying the descriptions is still time consuming. We'd like to be able to leverage an API where we could pass the image  - or more like set of images - to TrironGPT and it would pass back the description.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Utilize AI to help negotiate terms and conditions in Procurement contracts
Description: Procurement often receives requests to agree to supplier's terms and conditions before an order can be placed. These terms can conflict with standard University PO terms, and extensive review is require to red line the supplier's terms in order to meet university requirements. These university requirements can be contained in various documents, including UC PO terms, Data Security requirements, Privacy requirements, GASB96 requirements, and Covered Services, among others. The ability of an AI application to review these requirements and compare them with proposed contract language from suppliers would save our Contract Managers valuable time, especially in the "first pass" stage of contract negotiation. UCOP and other campuses have expressed an interest is this type of technology as well.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Triton GPT as a Student Class Schedule Builder
Description: Leverage data integrations and TGPT's ability to help students build their quarterly class schedule.  This will be used to assist students in enrolling in classes, checking for time conflicts, checking for finals conflicts etc. (pls see attached requirements list - not all requirements are hard-requirements).  TGPT can already do many of these functions (with outdated class schedule information) - we would like to extend these capabilities using current class schedule, pre-requisite, course repeat/restriction, and finals schedule data.  Requirements can be found at:  https://ucsdcloud-my.sharepoint.com/:x:/g/personal/dgarrison_ucsd_edu/EV8GZnfqwpBPiR3_Mp_Fiw8BBZ07kgy9z_GphGeTY0Jz2A?e=raXPPR

Purpose
Reduce time spent on the transcript validation process

Description
An AI-driven document analysis tool that validates grades, verifies fulfillment of provision requirements, and identifies transferable coursework from student transcript submissions

Current State
- Admissions officers currently spend significant time in the transcript validation process on routine tasks such as information verification and manual course detail entry
- They also spend a substantial amount of time on communication with students regarding incomplete or incorrect transcript submissions
- An AI-enabled solution can automate routine administrative tasks such as grade validation, provision fulfillment verification, and course detail entry, as well as perform more complex analyses such as identifying transferable coursework and generating student communications
- This solution shifts Admissions Officers' role to primarily monitoring and exception handling, freeing them to focus on higher-value student support activities

Potential Impact
- Reduces processing time and increases transcript validation throughput
- Frees staff from routine administrative tasks, allowing reallocation to higher-value student support activities
- Improves handling of non-articulated or out-of-state courses
- Reduces student wait times, potentially improving the enrollment experience

A tool that compares applicant-submitted courses with transferability guidelines and program-specific major prep requirements, verifying whether the courses meet necessary criteria.

Key points:
- Prospective transfer students select one major and one alternative major when submitting their application. Currently, admissions staff must research the major to understand the required courses, and then manually check the applicant’s course history, course by course, to confirm if all requirements are met
- Manually entering coursework for incomplete or inaccurate applications increases workload
- Multiple different systems and windows are used simultaneously to execute the process (TES, Assist.org, etc.)
- There is reported difficulty in evaluating major prep for non-articulated courses and coursework from four-year or out-of-state institutions

An AI-driven document analysis tool that verifies whether a student’s submission (e.g., transcript or application) is complete and adheres to formatting and content requirements

Key points:
- A significant number of student application and transcript submissions currently are incomplete or fail to meet requirements (>41% of submissions for some processes)
- Admissions Officers spend significant time communicating with students and manually entering data to resolve issues
- An AI-driven checker can verify submissions instantly and alert students when their submissions are incomplete or incorrect
- By ensuring only complete and compliant submissions advance to the full application review process, the solution saves both time and effort for staff and students, cutting down on manual interventions and accelerating cycle times

Short Name of Idea/Solution: COI Office AI Project: UC San Diego Personal Assistant for Guidance on Engagements (PAGE) for Faculty and Researchers
Description:
Domestic and International collaborations are vital to UC San Diego’s research ecosystem. UC San Diego has been at the forefront and integral to understanding that the progress of science is based on interdisciplinary and collaborative. The current process is unclear and cumbersome.  In order to ensure success, UC San Diego must provide Researchers a tool to provide both guidance, actionable steps and a risk assessment of a collaboration or outside activity.

UC San Diego Researchers are requested to collaborate and engage with other researchers every day and around the world. Examples of these foreign and domestic engagements could be answering questions about a most recent publication, co-authoring a paper, sharing scientific materials or data, full scale U.S. government funded projects to visiting appointments at other universities, international scientific centers between universities, and everything else in between (i.e., teaching, educational programmatic development, sabbatical, etc.). Given the volume of UCSD researchers and the number of opportunities they are presented with, we need a sustainable and real-time solution to guide and analyze the risk level of the potential engagement and continued updates to the Researcher on new regulations or updates.

The Use Case:  
A UC San Diego Researcher with active federal funding has been invited by an outside entity (foreign or domestic) to present on their research, to collaborate on research, potentially be offered a visiting appointment, offered access to resources, and/or to teach. Primary questions when receiving such an invitation from Researchers are: 
•	What is permissible? 
•	Is prior approval required?
•	What system(s) is used for review and approval? 
•	When and who needs to be informed at UC San Diego and externally? 
•	Will the engagement impact current or future federal funding? 
•	What is considered a Malign Foreign Talent Recruitment Program (MFTRP), prohibited by federal agencies? 

Current Challenges: 
At this time, UC San Diego Researchers must review many websites and/or contact many central offices (such as, Departmental support or ARC, Academic Personnel Services, the COI Office, specific federal agency requirements, International Engagement, Travel, Risk Management, etc.) to fully understand all processes, IT systems, and policies to determine whether an invitation by an outside entity (foreign or domestic) is permissible, whether prior approval is required, what systems are used, and which offices must provide approval. Researchers must be informed of the risk levels of a potential collaboration, how to mitigate risks if necessary, and when to be transparent and disclose these collaborations within UC San Diego and to federal agencies. Most collaborations are considered low to medium risk and therefore UCSD researchers shouldn’t have to spend time reviewing websites or consulting with central offices.  There is currently no efficient and effective solution.
Why do UC San Diego Researchers need to be mindful about collaborations?
Since 2018, foreign influence reviews with the National Institutes of Health (NIH), NSPM-33, and the CHIPS and Science Act (prohibition of Malign FTRPs), taught educational institutions that collaborations (on behalf of UCSD or as an outside activity) with non-U.S. entities requires disclosure, due diligence, and scrutiny. Researchers must consider a risk-based approach when considering collaborations, because of the potential that the collaboration could have a damaging effect and cause foreign interference to the U.S. research enterprise.  UC San Diego already experienced the effect in 2018; and it must provide meaningful and supportive guidance to our UCSD Researchers in real-time when the Researcher is being invited to a collaborative opportunity. 

Why do we need UCSD AI PAGE? 
It is imperative that UCSD identify potential risks, so as not to inadvertently create an environment that halts international collaborations due to a Researcher’s confusion or lack of awareness on how to manage potential research security concerns. The UCSD AI PAGE tool will provide the supportive guidance, actionable steps and real-time mitigation when the UCSD Researcher is considering potential collaborative opportunities (within UCSD or independently as an outside activity) within a closed and private AI model. As new regulations and settlements become deployed, the UCSD AI PAGE tool will query all past interactions with a UCSD Researcher and produce a list of Researchers that should receive new and updated information (i.e. 1286 list FY22 released in 2023, and FY23 released in 2024). This with protect and provide immediate support to the Researcher while minimizing UCSD’s risk posture. 

In addition to UCSD AI PAGE’s ability to review and provide high level guidance on Export Controls regulations and sanctions on foreign countries, the U.S. Government has further expanded their concerns with countries of concern from China, Iran, North Korea, and Russia.  The U.S. Government has required Universities to assess if the research is considered within the definition of Critical Emerging Technologies (CET) the list of which is vastly broad and extensive. 

Given the inclusion by the U.S. government in 2023 of the prohibition of Malign FTRPs, UCSD researchers must be informed on what engagement risks may impact the ability to accept federal funding for their research. Since many engagement opportunities are not considered MFRTPs, UCSD researchers should not deny themselves these engagement opportunities. UCSD researchers need instant guidance, awareness, education, and the next steps of disclosure/regulations steps that include a clear risk level. Most opportunities are low risk, and Researchers need the support and guidance quickly and simply. The high risks can come from a country of concern, an entity or program on the 1286 list (from the Chips and Science Act), critical emerging technology, controlled technology, a sanctioned country, or include characteristics of an Malign FTRP, etc.  The high-risk determinations from UCSD AI PAGE would require the Researcher to schedule a meeting with RCI Research Security. 

The UCSD AI PAGE at its infancy will answer fundamental questions like, “I am a faculty member with federal research and have been invited to speak at x university (non-US entity) is this permissible and what do I need to do to disclose?” The results would provide the researcher with the policies and actionable steps required while identifying risk level and mitigation steps. 

The UCSD AI PAGE will diminish redundant reviews across many UCSD offices: UCSD AI PAGE would assist UCSD Researchers, departments, academic personnel (COC policies), the Conflict of Interest Office, RCI Research Security, Research Integrity and Oversight (RIO), International Offices (GI, international office), Export Control Office, risk management, travel, overall compliance, etc.

We have many systems for expenses at UC San Diego, and knowing the right system and required documentation for each use case can be difficult to manage. Guided Buying, as an interactive TritonGPT assistant, could close this gap. In Guided Buying, TritonGPT can display a series of prompts that guides users through a series of relevant questions/decision points to help them understand 1) the process for procuring or paying for what they're buying, 2) which system to do it with, 3) any relevant requirements/special conditions that may apply (ex. If trying to contract a service that a covered-employee also performs, you need sign off from Labor Relations first. Or, if you're buying a new piece of software, the Privacy and Security Teams need to be involved first). This would reduce training/ramp up time for new employees, reduce Services and Support questions, and reduce the percentage of transactions that are sent back for revisions due to not including the necessary information/documentation up front.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Cross reference Design Guidelines and Specifications
Description:
Steven's Note: Not sure what Walter's VC Area is, so this was my best guess.

We have a 250+ page design guideline and thousands of pages of specifications that could be used as a LLM to cross check design submittals. Certain aspects are required design criteria that could be identified for all projects and use AI for preliminary reviews savings reviews by multiple SME's for initial pass. SME review is still required for unique design solutions but in general this could save substantial time on flagging items that don't follow standards.

With the launch of Llama 3.2, 90B, we have the capability for TGPT to recognize the contents of an image file. Early tests show that it has the capability to "analyze" a chart or graph and help provide insights into the data being visualized. There are likely a host of other use cases. It may be worth starting with an assistant designed for this purpose then determining whether the "vision" capabilities can be folded into the other assistants.

We've had anecdotal requests wondering if TGPT can search the internet and bring back natural language responses, similar to Perplexity. Now that Danswer supports tool calling, we have the ability to do that and have identified a low cost utility that will perform this.
Copy and paste the contents of an email into an assistant and get an analysis and risk score of how likely the email is to be a phishing attempt.

Integrated Procure-to-Pay Solutions (IPPS) handles a large number of inquiries from both internal and external clients that are vital to the university's day-to-day functioning (approx 40,000/year). IPPS's tier 1 support queues take up around 43.35% of our total case volume within Services & Support. Our agents are able to manage an average of 95 cases per week but face overutilization due to other projects and tasks that are associated with their job card. The case count stands around 148 per queue which exceeds capacity by 55.79% and impedes on our ability to resolve cases within our 24 hour desired window. When queues are backed up, this can lead to a ripple effect, causing dissatisfaction among the university's suppliers, delays in research projects due to missing goods, and other significant consequences.

Our solution is to look for a virtual agent that can support our case volume and reduce the amount of cases that are sent to our teams. The virtual agent would be available in the hours our agents are unable to work, answer the status questions, and provide instructions to clients seeking to understand our processes better. This will significantly reduce the workload of human agents, resulting in tangible cost savings by eliminating the need to hire extra resources. Our agents would also be able to work on other initiatives such as outreach to clients and optimizing training material. We are in the process of searching for a virtual agent through an RFI with other stakeholders in the university to ensure we are sourcing the appropriate solution. In addition to our research, we have begun to identify resources that need to be updated, which should lighten the lift of preparation during the implementation stage. We hope to procure a virtual agent in the future or similar solution that allows us to respond to our inquiries sooner and provide a better experience to our clients.

Create a TritonGPT Task-Specific AI Assistant that could take a faculty member’s current BioBib document (in the correct UC format as a PDF or Word document) and place the information properly into the Interfolio Faculty180 tool which can then output the information in a variety of desired formats (BioBib style, NSF style, NIH style, etc.).  This would be extremely helpful in getting all of our faculty BioBibs into Interfolio in a way that promotes transparency throughout the academic review process.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: TGPT sandbox for developing proof-of-concept business solutions
Description:
Several of us at the SIO-BI team have been following closely the TGPT development since its inception and "playing" around with it.   Similarly, we've also been exploring Gemini, MS Co-pilot, ChatGPT and looking for productivity gains where possible.  Lately, I've been exploring semantic search as a potential tool to help in two separate use cases and have run into the issue of data security.  In one case, utilizing unstructured Academic Personnel data, we have P2 and P3 data so we need to tokenize and prompt for it in a secure environment.  We have an entire database of historical data and are obviously not a candidate for existing tools since we need both, a safe repository for the tokenized data and a secure LLM which will not use said data for training purposes, or will clearly state the data will be deleted within a particular time frame.  

Since TGPT is housed in a safe, closed and secure environment at the SDSC, we would like to know if there are plans for allow UCSD Divisions, Department to have access to the same infrastructure.  Ideally, a sandbox scenario where we could store tokens, ie. a vector database, and a Llama LLM would provide enough to develop proof-of-concept business solutions in the short term.   If we have success we may be able to build our own environment for a production level solution since stakeholders would see the benefits and fund it.   Of course, we would cover set up costs for a sandbox at SDSC if that option was available.

Robotic Process Automation involves software bots and AI agents automating repetitive tasks. In an effort to increase transaction efficiency and reduce the resources required, RPA could automate tasks that aren't candidates for normal integrations or aren't likely to be prioritized by central IT resources. 

IPPS processes more than 1.3M transactions a year, and while we've automated the vast majority of them, we still have many time consuming manual processes. I can think of more than a dozen processes IPPS could automate with RPA - and imagine there are many, many more possible examples across campus. Some examples of IPPS processes we haven't been able to automate in other ways  - 1) issuing new P-Cards/Travel & Entertainment cards in the US Bank portal after the new card request in Concur is fully approved, 2) automatically reducing the P-Card/T&E Card limit for cardholders who haven't reconciled within policy requirements - and then increasing the limit if/when they do, 3) enter wire payments into the bank portal after the SNOW request form is fully approved, 4) automate the process of moving checks that haven't been cashed in 6 months to the unclaimed accounts, 5) Setting up new gift card sub-teams in Tremendous for human subject payments after the associated PO is fully approved/issued, etc.

Several other higher eds have presented on their recent successes implementing RPA - here's an example: https://s3.amazonaws.com/bee-content/beeUploadedFiles/33501097/44310b96-8e1c-4657-bb9a-8572374e8f86_StreamliningProcurementHarnessingthePowerofRoboticProcessAutomation.pdf

Short Name of Idea/Solution: AppZen for Concur Expense Report Auditing
Description:
Problem: Expense Reports (for travel, events, P-Card, or misc. employee reimbursement) require strict review to ensure compliance with policy and guard against fraud. Policies are complex, and expense reports typically involve after-the-fact reviews, which complicate review and can delay approvals. Pierre has challenged us to reduce the amount of manual reviews we do to only 10% of travel transactions  - and auto approve 90%. We've managed to increase out auto-approval percentage from 46% to 73% in the last FY, but struggle to find additional areas we can bypass review on without exposing the university to too much risk . 

Potential Solution: AppZen (https://www.appzen.com/expense-audit-software) is a 3rd party application that integrates with Concur and runs all expense reports against dynamic rules and uses AI (OCR, NLP) to review receipts, compare expenses across different users and transactions to prevent duplicate/fraudulent reimbursements, and more to quickly (within 15 mins) analyze the risk of each expense as low, medium, or high. Only expenses that are high risk require manual review, and over time the system learns how we typically handle different risks and learns to automate review even further. 

We've had a demo and are impressed, and are trying to learn about their pricing model.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Closed Circuit AI Assistant for Ethics & Compliance
Description:
TritonGPT closed circuit AI Assistant for Ethics & Compliance, to be trained on specific UC policies related to assessment and processing of misconduct allegations (UC Whistleblower Policy, Whistleblower Protection Policy, Sexual Violence & Sexual Harassment Policy, Anti-discrimination Policy, Abusive Conduct Policy, etc.).  AI Assistant would be used by the investigative staff managing high-risk investigations to ask specific questions across these policies and would be used to process time intensive investigation work.  For example, the AI Assistant would be provided investigator notes from witness interviews and could provide a witness summary for inclusion in an investigative report that references the appropriate policy language and meets the formatting/template requirements of the office. Output would need to be verified by the investigator, but the time savings is anticipated to be substantial.  This would allow investigative staff to focus on higher level skills and judgment in addressing misconduct, freeing them up from administrative and process minutiae that can be processed by AI.  

Character limitations would need to be higher than current restrictions on TritonGPT to allow for submission of larger amounts of information for summarization. Utilization would be restricted to data types and levels currently approved for use with the AI.

Problem: We have 70,000 (non-employee) suppliers/payees in our supplier master. Each supplier/payee we add takes time to setup, requires maintenance, and introduces risk into the university. The ideal behavior would be to source goods/services from existing suppliers when something is needed, but there's not currently a good way to identify what the existing suppliers sell/provide.  

Idea: Create a tool that campus can use to search across all payees/suppliers in Oracle. What they sell can be sourced through past orders/payments to them by others, and NPL can help interpret what the user is looking for - even if they don't use the exact same terms. We could give weight/preference in search results to UC agreement suppliers, or local/diverse/small businesses - and even potentially pair it with information from Blink that gives additional guidance on certain high risk commodities (like events, security services, etc).  We had a similar tool pre-ESR, the Marketplace Supplier Search Tool, which was homegrown. At that time, we asked payees what they sold during registration, and used that to match potential suppliers for campus but it was too dependent on matching exact terms that suppliers vs. campus used. I feel like AI could really improve that experience, without too much effort.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Lab, Experiment, and Procurement Efficiencies: Accelerating Experiment Life Cycle for Research
Description:
This is a joint exploration between Jacob's School of Engineering's Laboratory for Emerging Intelligence and IPPS which consists of a series of related AI solutions that result in researchers being able to get from an idea for an experiment to actually performing the research more quickly. Jacobs is pursuing a financial gift from Fisher Scientific, in the aims of accelerating research. Project scope includes: 
1) Lab and Experimental Data Intelligence 
2) Lab and Procurement Analytics 
3) Lab Efficiencies and Guided Buying 
4) Virtual Lab Management

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Guided Buying/P2P Orchestration (Oro Labs Proof of Concept)
Description: We have many systems for expenses at UC San Diego, and knowing the right system and required documentation for each use case can be difficult to manage. Guided Buying, a single front door for all P2P activities that then directs users to the right place, could close this gap. Guided Buying uses a combination of AI (NPL, GenAI) to take a basic description of what someone is trying to do, and then guide them through a series of relevant questions/decision points to get them to the correct place. Fully fleshed out, it could also serve as solution for P2P Orchestration - providing not only a guide but a sophisticated piece of middleware that serves as a single intake portal and then facilitates integrations between all P2P systems to create/submit/approve/etc. various transactions. With over 1.3 million transactions taking place in the P2P space every year, the ability to consolidate and streamline these processes could lead to significant time savings and a reduction in administrative burden.

Purpose
Streamline and optimize application and transcript review processes, while maintaining or improving accuracy and fairness.

Description
A process improvement initiative has been started to streamline the application and transcript review processes. After eliminating inefficiencies, AI can be leveraged to assist in reviewing by automating validation of binary criteria (criteria met/not met) and supporting with components that require interpretation. 

Key Points
- Three review processes could benefit from AI support:​
       - Holistic review for new 1st year students​
       - Review of transfer applications​
       - Validation of existing student transcripts​
- Reviews are time-intensive and 200 external readers are hired annually to conduct these reviews​
- The number of submissions requiring review is expected to continue growing​
- An AI solution has the potential to significantly reduce time spent per review through automation and intelligent assistance​

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Smart form using AI to streamline the process for retaining a contractor
Description:
We’re trying to create a smart form to help guide a requestor through the decision process to determine if an individual they want to retain is a contractor or an employee. Wayde and I were discussing the potential of AI to help with this complex process and he recommended I reach out to you. Here’s our problem statement:
 
•	When someone makes a request from Procurement for an individual to support work on campus, they need to affirmatively determine (by answering a series of questions) that the work is indeed outside the scope of work an employee would typically provide. Recent legislation has created additional ambiguity and made the decision process even more difficult to navigate effectively.
 
And our proposed solution:
•	Use AI to guide the requestor through the decision process and provide a solution with a high level of confidence.


I’m sharing a high-level decision tree as a starting point: https://lucid.app/lucidspark/invitations/accept/inv_f6a49815-6525-4d98-8c22-b75e37a744e1

Samson Blackwell
Senior Director, Talent Solutions

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Batch or auto approval of low-value reimbursements in Concur
Description: Batch or auto approval of low-value reimbursements in Concur. Even low-value transactions require multiple levels of approval in Concur. Could we either batch those low-value transactions so that they may be approved with one click and/or have AI evaluate the history of the submitter to determine which transactions are low-risk. Several business officers (Pamela Fruge, Jon Host and Jessica Kuhrt)have submitted screen recordings to Allorah as an example of the multiple clicks required to approve these low-value transactions.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Add APM and APPM to TritonGPT
Description:
Add the APM and Academic Personnel Process Manual (APPM) to TritonGPT so that those who support faculty may have a resource to quickly find answers to policy and process questions.
APM: https://www.ucop.edu/academic-personnel-programs/academic-personnel-policy/index.html and https://adminrecords.ucsd.edu/ppm/docs/230-000.html
APPM: https://aps.ucsd.edu/tools/ap_process_manual.html

Using TritonGPT or a similar LLM, Assessment Builder allows instructors to upload the corpus of their Canvas LMS course, and create exam and quiz assessments tailored to their course content and materials. Numerous assessment types are supported, including knowledge checks, multiple choice tests, short answer exams, and written essay prompts.  This saves instructor time, creates assessments that directly measure comprehension of course content, and helps create learning objectives for new courses.

AI Study Aide can leverage UC San Diego’s lecture capture system to create class summaries, study guides, and new ways to interact with course content. TritonGPT or a similar secure LLM could train on audio and video of individual class sessions each week, plus the entire audio/video series of past courses. This content could be transformed to create summaries for both students and instructors.  Ultimately, the content could serve as a real-time AI chat bot, guiding students to learn important course concepts, and saving TA time.

Recognizing that routine machine auto-captioning is often subpar, Accessibility Enhancer improves lecture capture captioning and transcription accuracy to the benefit of all students. Open source AI like WhisperAI can process the recorded audio of individual class sessions each week, plus the entire audio/video series of past courses. Our pilots have shown that captioning accuracy is immediately improved with this AI treatment, and can be improved further as the AI models further "learn" the audio and speaking characteristics of individual instructors.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Want to be able to use AI to analyze some of our student data in a secure environment that does not leave UCSD
Description: Initial use case would just be to use a LLM prompt to ask questions in a secure environment that does not leave UCSD. If we could ask the prompt questions with FERPA data that would be even better.

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Turn natural language question into a SQL query that can retrieve meaningful information from existing data sources, using Wolfram.
Description:
The current data querying and reporting process at UC San Diego involves querying various repositories of crucial data, including various activity hubs. The Activity Hubs are comprised of 5 primary data domains: Employee, Facilities, Financial, Research, and Student. Today application developers and analysts build variety of Staff, Faculty, and Student application / reporting solutions based on activity hubs using a variety of tools. Hundreds of people across UCSD, if not thousands, build or generate reports today. Thousands of people query structured data or are presented with structured data in many UC San Diego applications. Today people have to either submit report request to a team or someone that can write reports or build reports themselves which can extend the amount of time it takes to get the information people are looking for. An AI solution that can turn natural language questions into an SQL query will free requestors time and data/BI analysts up to solve more complex problems or build more involved reports. 
1: Using an LLM / Wolfram, turn a natural language question into a SQL query that can retrieve values and rows (Simple SQL fact retrieval)
2: Using an LLM / Wolfram, visualize, summarize, format results from a query
3: Using an LLM / Wolfram, put together data science packages that can be repeated by a suitably skilled analyst
4: Using an LLM / Wolfram, bring content and data external to the university and within Wolfram knowledge bases that may relate to analytic topics

Short Name of Idea/Solution: Build arguments for legal cases using Google scholar
Description:
Index the UC legal case settlements from Google Scholar data, use the data from successful cases to build arguments for UC San Diego legal cases. 
Note: Adding UC legal cases to the AI system has great, but unproven, potential. We may not be able to assess its value until the system was up and running and we could test it. What is involved is scraping Google Scholar to get the text of the legal cases (there is no copyright issue because the text of the cases are not owned by Google) and then training a persona of the AI on those texts. The level of effort would need to be determined in order to prioritize for implementation.

RobinAI for contract review in the Office of Contracts and Grants, see: https://www.robinai.com/
Problem statement:
Impact and proposed outcome:
Solution summary:

GASB 96 provides a framework for assessing whether a subscription-based information technology arrangement falls within its scope. The evaluation involves considering the nature of the arrangement and whether it meets certain control criteria. This process occurs monthly once list of new POs received. It is a highly manual process. Failure to correctly flag contracts could result in an audit, corrections, and a deeper dive to see if other contracts were missed. During the assessment 94 contracts were reviewed, 4 were GSB 96 compliant contracts, the average time to review (process and wait time) was 1-2.5 hours, 1.5 FTE are needed for contract compliance. GenAI can be used to: 1) revew contract PDF and PNG files to extract needed text; 2) accurately calculate and categorize relevant expenses throughout contract; 3) review large contracts; 4) ensure security. Short Name of Idea/Solution: International Students Program Office Assistant (ISPO Assistant) using RoBERTa
Description: The ISPO Assistant leverages the RoBERTa technology and provides efficient document processing within the International Students & Programs Office(ISPO), provides a support system for International students, and addresses concerns related to their academic and visa issues. Specifically, it simplifies the process and turn-around time for international students pursuing work/study internships through a sponsored employer. The current process includes hours of manual review of supporting documents and approvals, the proposed solution uses Generative AI to scan documents to highlight text from employers about their hiring of International students in work-study programs. The solution will alleviate staff in VCSA in the ISPO office from having to read these documents and develop reports for visa reporting for students. The initial proof of concept resulted in 94% accuracy and processed 200 documents in 30 seconds. Reduced 4hrs of work to 2 mins and students get their approvals in 1 day vs. 1 week. More information: recorded presentation starts at the 6-minute mark: https://mediaspace.ucsd.edu/media/AI+Use+Case+Community+7-14-2023/1_vvaxmsui

Fund Manager Coach is built on TritonGPT and recognizing the crucial role of Fund Managers in overseeing grants and managing departmental finances, this assistant will enhance their understanding of UC San Diego's financial policies and procedures. Fund Manager Coach is trained on documentation that supports how to develop budgets for research proposals, advising faculty on federal grant guidelines, reviewing and approving financial transactions, managing payroll, ensuring that federal guidelines are being followed during the course of grant spending, and other financial duties. More information: https://blink.ucsd.edu/technology/ai/tritongpt/index.html

Job Description Helper is built on top of TritonGPT, and is engineered to streamline the job description creation process for hiring managers. Leveraging over 1,300 career tracks job standard templates, it uses a pre-defined flow that engages hiring managers in a dialogue, capturing the specific requirements of the job. The AI then crafts language that not only complies with established job card standards but also accurately reflects the unique characteristics of the position. This feature significantly reduces the time and effort involved in drafting job descriptions, ensuring they are both precise and tailored to the individual needs of the role. More information: https://blink.ucsd.edu/technology/ai/tritongpt/index.html

Short Name of Idea/Solution: TritonGPT - UC San Diego's overall chat assistant
Description:
TritonGPT is a generative AI solution built on an advanced open-source large language model (LLM) that is capable of answering a wide range of questions about UC San Diego. It can help users navigate UC San Diego's policies, procedures, and campus life. It can be used as a personal assistant as follows:.

* Ask UC San Diego Related Questions: Pose questions like "What is the policy on employee travel reimbursement?" or "What are some good restaurants on campus?" TritonGPT will provide detailed and relevant information.
* Content Generation: Need help with content creation? Try commands like "Generate an outline for a presentation slide deck based on <insert topic>" or Produce an email to thank my employees for <insert what you are thankful for>.
* Document Summarization: Copy and paste documents or articles related to UC San Diego, then ask TritonGPT to summarize the content. It's a time-saving feature for extracting key information.
* Content Editing: Utilize TritonGPT for editing and refining content related to UC San Diego. It's a valuable tool for polishing emails, reports, or any written material.
* Seek feedback and suggestions: TritonGPT can provide feedback and suggestions to help you improve your work processes and procedures. You can ask questions like "What are some ways I can improve my communication skills in the workplace?" or "Are there any suggestions for streamlining our team's workflow?"
* Ask for Recommendations: Seeking recommendations for UC San Diego events, study spots, or local hangouts? TritonGPT has you covered.

More information about TritonGPT: https://blink.ucsd.edu/technology/ai/tritongpt/index.html
